verdict_reasoning: B2M (beta-2-microglobulin) is a stable, non-covalent partner of the MHC class I heavy chain (a type I transmembrane protein). Although B2M itself has no transmembrane domain, it is assembled intracellularly with the HLA alpha-chain and co-trafficked as a constitutive surface complex to the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane on virtually all nucleated cells. B2M lies on the extracellular face beside the alpha-3 domain, fully accessible from outside the cell. This is a textbook stable_complex_partner topology; the dissociation into soluble serum B2M is a downstream consequence of complex turnover, not evidence against surface residency. The pMHC scope exclusion does not apply here — it covers intracellular proteins whose only surface story is peptide MHC-presentation; B2M is itself an extracellular structural subunit of the MHC I complex. Anti-B2M therapeutic programs (e.g., in multiple myeloma) confirm cell-surface targeting.
